  • pyRANN TUTORIAL
pyrann
  • pyRANN PACKAGE
  • View page source

pyRANN PACKAGE

load(…)

Loads in a poscar file, LAMMPS data file, LAMMPS dump file, or cfg file and assigns the corresponding atoms, box, types, elements, number of atoms, and timestep to a system object.

system(…)

A class representing a physical system with atoms, box dimensions, and various properties.

series(…)

A class to represent a series of atomistic systems, which can be exported to various file formats.

calibration

Calibration module.

processing(…)

A class to obtain atomic descriptors for RANN, MTP, and GAP formalisms.
load(filename, filetype=None, timestep=None, series=False)

Loads in a poscar file, LAMMPS data file, LAMMPS dump file, or cfg file and assigns the corresponding atoms, box, types, elements, number of atoms, and timestep to a system object. Stresses and forces will be added soon

Parameters:

  • filename (str) – A string representation of the desired file to be loaded.

  • filetype (str | None (default: None)) – Type of file to be loaded. Supported filetypes are poscar, data, dump, and cfg. If no filetype is given, it will be inferred from the filename

  • timestep (str | int | ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – A string or integer representation of the timestep for the atomic system being loaded. Defaults to 0.

  • series (bool (default: False)) – Boolean value to determine if the file being loaded contains multiple timesteps.

Returns:

System Object or Series Object

A system object if series == False, otherwise a series object

Raises:

ValueError – If the filetype is unsupported.

class system(box=None, atoms=None, types=None, elements=None, natoms=None, timestep=None, energy=None, stress=None, force=None, descriptor=None)

A class representing a physical system with atoms, box dimensions, and various properties.

Initializes a system with atom positions, types, box dimensions, and other optional properties.

Parameters:

  • box (ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – The 3x3 matrix representing the simulation box. Default is the identity matrix.

  • atoms (ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – A 3xN array representing the atom positions. This is required.

  • types (str | int | ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – Atom types, either as a string, integer, or list. Default is an array of ones.

  • elements (str | list | None (default: None)) – Elements corresponding to atom types. If None, elements are generated.

  • natoms (str | int | None (default: None)) – Number of atoms in the system. If None, derived from atoms.

  • timestep (str | int | None (default: None)) – The current timestep of the simulation. Default is 0.

  • energy (str | float | None (default: None)) – The energy of the system in \(eV\).

  • stress (str | ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – A 3x3 stress tensor array. Default is None.

  • force (str | ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – A 3xN force array. Default is None.

  • descriptor (str | None (default: None)) – A description of the system. Default is None.

Raises:

ValueError – If atoms is not provided.

Attributes

atoms

Returns (3, natoms) shaped np.ndarray of the atom positions.

box

Returns the system's box vectors.

descriptor

Returns a description of the system.

elements

Returns the element types of the system.

energy

Returns the energy of the system

force

Returns the x, y, and z force components of each atom if applicable.

natoms

Returns the number of atoms in the system.

stress

Returns the stress tensor of the system if applicable.

timestep

Returns the timestep.

types

Returns the atom types.

Methods

change_type(…)

Changes the integer type for selected atoms.

createsupercell(…)

Creates a supercell by replicating the atoms in the original unit cell and placing them within a new box.

export(…)

Exports the system data to a specified file in a given format.

gsfe(…)

Generates a series of simulations for computing the Generalized Stacking Fault Energy.

solidsolution(…)

Adds a solid solution to the atomic system by introducing solute atoms into the lattice.

strain(…)

Strains the simulation cell in even increments from -maximum_strain to maximum_strain.

surface(…)

Creates a surface by modifying the atomic system and box dimensions based on a specified direction.

thermal(…)

Simulates thermal perturbations on the atomic system by applying random displacements and box strain.

vacancy(…)

Removes atoms from the system to create vacancies by removing atoms close to randomly selected points.
change_type(new_types)

Changes the integer type for selected atoms.

Changes the integer atom type for the system object. “new_types” should be array-like where the first position represents the new type for current atom type 1. For example, if a system contains Ti as type and Al as type 2, they could be flipped using “new_types=[2,1]”. “new_types” should have the same length as the desired number of atom types.

Parameters:

new_types (ArrayLike | int) – An array-like item that represents new atom types.

Returns:

None – This method updates the system’s types attribute.

createsupercell(new_box=None)

Creates a supercell by replicating the atoms in the original unit cell and placing them within a new box.

This method takes the original box and atom positions and generates a new supercell with atoms placed according to the new box dimensions. It accounts for periodic boundary conditions and generates a new set of atom types and positions based on the replication.

Parameters:

new_box (ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – Column vector array of new box dimensions.

Returns:

None – This method updates the system’s attributes: types, atoms, box, and natoms based on the new supercell.

Raises:

ValueError – If any unexpected issues arise during supercell creation.

export(filename, filetype=None, style=None)

Exports the system data to a specified file in a given format.

This method allows the user to export the system’s atoms and box dimensions to various file formats, such as POSCAR, LAMMPS data, and others. The user can choose the output file’s name, type, and coordinate style (e.g., Cartesian, direct, or crystal).

Parameters:

  • filename (str) – String of the filename to write.

  • filetype (str | None (default: None)) – String of the file extension. Not needed if extension is given in filetype. filetypes are ‘poscar’, ‘pos’, ‘data’, ‘dat’, ‘dump’, ‘qe’, ‘quantum_espresso’, ‘vasp’, or ‘INCAR’ Note: Some of these filetypes may not be implemented yet.

  • style (str | None (default: None)) – String of type of atom coordinates desired (direct, crystal, or cartesian).

Returns:

None – This method writes the system data to the specified file in the given format.

Raises:

ValueError – If an invalid filetype or style is provided.

gsfe(nsims=100, direction=array([[1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]]), reps=array([1, 1, 12]))

Generates a series of simulations for computing the Generalized Stacking Fault Energy.

This method creates a series of simulations by generating different configurations of a system’s atomic positions, shifting the positions and box based on specified replication parameters. It uses the makelattice3 method to construct the lattice and applies transformations to simulate different strain states or surface configurations. The direction should be a 3x3 matrix with x, y, and z as column vectors.

Parameters:

  • nsims (int | str (default: 100)) – The total number of unique structures to generate moving along the fault.

  • direction (ArrayLike (default: array([[1, 0, 0],        [0, 1, 0],        [0, 0, 1]]))) – Array of column vectors representing the miller indicies of the given simulation cell. The stacking fault plane should be the x direction.

  • reps (ArrayLike (default: array([ 1,  1, 12]))) – An array of size (1,3) representing the number of replications of the final simulation cell in the x, y, and z directions.

Returns:

series – A series object containing the configurations generated in each simulation, with atomic positions, box, and types.

Raises:

Exception – If the direction vectors lead to an invalid configuration (i.e., a left-handed coordinate system).

Notes

The method iteratively modifies the atomic positions and the simulation box to generate different configurations. It also creates a series of simulations that can be used to analyze the GSFE by comparing different atomic configurations under strain. The final series of configurations is returned as a series object.

solidsolution(solute_type=None, num_atoms=1, solute_element=None, num_neighbors=1)

Adds a solid solution to the atomic system by introducing solute atoms into the lattice.

This method randomly selects a position in the system and changes the type of num_neighbor closest atoms corresponding to the solute. The solute atoms are added to the system’s atomic types and elements.

Parameters:

  • solute_type (int (default: None)) – Integer value for the atom type of the solute.

  • num_atoms (int (default: 1)) – Integer value for the number of solute atoms.

  • solute_element (str | int | None (default: None)) – Element type of the solute.

  • num_neighbors (int (default: 1)) – Integer value representing the number of neighbors to a random point to change to the solute type.

Returns:

None – The method modifies the types attribute of the atomic system by assigning the selected solute type to the chosen nearest neighbor atoms.

Raises:

ValueError – If the solute type or element is not valid.

strain(strain_points=100, maximum_strain=0.05)

Strains the simulation cell in even increments from -maximum_strain to maximum_strain.

Strains the simulation cell in even increments up to “maximum_strain” in the following ways: volumetric strain, strain along x, strain along y, strain along z, yz shear strain, xz shear strain, xy shear strain, strain along (111), xy in-plane strain, xy in-plane shear strain, and global strain. Strain points indicates how many different simulation cells to create for each type of strain. The “maximum_strain” parameter is the decimal form of a percentage (0.1 for 10% maximum strain).

Parameters:

  • strain_points (int (default: 100)) – The number of unique structures to create for each strain mode (14 strain modes currently used?)

  • maximum_strain (float64 (default: 0.05)) – Maximum percentage (in decimal format) of the lattice parameter of the given simulation cell to strain the box.

Returns:

A series class object

surface(direction)

Creates a surface by modifying the atomic system and box dimensions based on a specified direction.

This method uses the input direction to define the orientation of the system, transforms the system to a new basis, and generates a supercell by filling the box with atoms. It then applies a transformation to the system’s atomic positions and box to create a surface-like structure. The result is a modified system with a new set of atoms and box dimensions.

Parameters:

direction (ArrayLike) – An array of column vectors representing the new simulation cell. Free surface will be on the x? plane.

Returns:

None – The method modifies the atoms and box attributes of the system in place.

Raises:

Exception – If the input direction results in an invalid configuration where the cross product leads to a zero vector.

thermal(temp, strain=0, nsims=100, N=None, ss_dict=None)

Simulates thermal perturbations on the atomic system by applying random displacements and box strain.

This method generates a series of configurations by thermally perturbing the atomic positions and box dimensions. The perturbations are applied to a base system, and the resulting atomic configurations are stored in a series for further analysis or export.

Parameters:

  • temp (str | int | float | None) – Maximum random perturbation of atoms. Currently, this should be a percentage of the given simulation cell’s lattice parameter (in decimal format).

  • strain (str | int | float | None (default: 0)) – Maximum strain given as a percentage (in decimal format) of the given simulation cell’s lattice parameter.

  • nsims (str | int | None (default: 100)) – The number of unique structures to produce.

  • N (int (default: None)) – Number of atoms to perturb. Useful if you only want to perturb a few random atoms.

  • ss_dict (dict | None (default: None)) – dictionary of keywords for doing thermal perturbation on solid solution. ss_keys = [‘solute_type’, ‘num_neighbors’, ‘num_atoms’]

Returns:

series – A series containing the generated configurations, each with perturbed atomic positions and box dimensions.

Raises:

ValueError – If the number of simulations (nsims) is not provided or invalid keywords are found in ss_dict.

vacancy(num_neighbors)

Removes atoms from the system to create vacancies by removing atoms close to randomly selected points.

This method randomly selects points within the simulation box and identifies the nearest neighbors to those points. It then removes a specified number of nearest neighbors from the atomic system, effectively creating vacancies in the structure.

Parameters:

num_neighbors (int) – The number of neighbors to delete from a randomly selected points.

Returns:

None – The method modifies the atoms and types attributes of the system in place, removing the specified number of atoms.

property atoms: ndarray

Returns (3, natoms) shaped np.ndarray of the atom positions.

Returns:

np.ndarray – A 3xN array representing the positions of the atoms.

property box: ndarray

Returns the system’s box vectors.

Returns:

np.ndarray (A 3x3 matrix representing the simulation box where x,) – y, and z are column vectors.

property descriptor: str | None

Returns a description of the system. Helpful to determine the file loaded from or operations done on the system.

Returns:

str – User defined string describing the system.

property elements: ArrayLike | None

Returns the element types of the system. If element types are unknown, the element type becomes “Type_n” where n is an integer representing the atom type.

Returns:

np.ndarray – Array of element types.

property energy: float64

Returns the energy of the system

Returns:

np.float64 – Float value of the energy.

property force: ArrayLike | None

Returns the x, y, and z force components of each atom if applicable. Returns None otherwise.

Returns:

np.ndarray – An array of shape (3, natoms) of the x, y, and z force components for each atom.

property natoms: int

Returns the number of atoms in the system.

Returns:

int – The number of atoms in the system.

property stress: ArrayLike | None

Returns the stress tensor of the system if applicable. Returns None otherwise.

Returns:

np.ndarray – A 3x3 matrix representing the stress tensor.

property timestep: int

Returns the timestep.

Returns:

int – The integer value of the timestep.

property types: ndarray

Returns the atom types.

Returns:

np.ndarray – A 1D array representing the atom types.

class series(systems=None, descriptor=None)

Bases: object

A class to represent a series of atomistic systems, which can be exported to various file formats.

Parameters:

  • systems (ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – A list or array of atomistic systems to be contained in the series.

  • descriptor (str | None (default: None)) – A descriptor to label the series (e.g., a string identifier).

Attributes

descriptor

Gets the descriptor for the series.

systems

Returns 1-D array of system objects in the series object

timesteps

Returns 1-D array of timesteps for all systems.

Methods

export(…)

Exports the systems in the series to a specified file format.
export(filename, directory=None, filetype=None, style=None)

Exports the systems in the series to a specified file format.

Parameters:

  • filename (str | None) – The name of the file to export (without extension).

  • directory (str | None (default: None)) – The directory where the file will be saved. If None, saves in the current directory.

  • filetype (str | None (default: None)) – The format of the file (e.g., ‘poscar’, ‘data’). If None, inferred from filename.

  • style (str | None (default: None)) – The style for representing atomic positions (e.g., ‘cartesian’, ‘direct’).

Raises:

ValueError – If an unsupported filetype is provided, or if the style is invalid.

Returns:

None

property descriptor: str

Gets the descriptor for the series.

Returns:

str – The current descriptor for the series.

property systems: ndarray

Returns 1-D array of system objects in the series object

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of system objects in the series object

property timesteps: ndarray

Returns 1-D array of timesteps for all systems.

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of timesteps.

class processing(input_file, formalism=None)

Bases: object

A class to obtain atomic descriptors for RANN, MTP, and GAP formalisms. Also used to investigate and refine databases and metaparameters for MLIPs

Parameters:

  • input_file (str) – The name (relative path) of the file that contains the ML potential information.

  • formalism (str | None (default: None)) – The formalism of the ML potential under investigation. Current options are RANN, MTP, and GAP (or SOAP). If not provided, it tries to infer based on input_file extension.

Attributes

features

Returns the features

formalism

Returns the current formalism.

global_sim_num

Returns array of the global simulation number each atom belongs to.

input_file

Returns a string representation of the input file.

local_id

Returns the local (within-simulation) atom id for each atom.

local_sim_num

Returns 1-D array of local (within-file) simulation numbers for each atom.

Methods

density()

Finds the average feature-space density for each simulation using the 100 nearest neighbors for each atom.

get_features(…)

Calculates and sets the features for the current formalism and input file.

get_umap(…)

Initializes UMAP for the set features.

plot(…)

Saves an interactive bokeh html plot to visualize the feature-space.

reduce(…)

Reduces the database by ${percent}%.
density()

Finds the average feature-space density for each simulation using the 100 nearest neighbors for each atom. Excludes atoms that belong to the same global simulation number.

Return type:

ndarray

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array (length nsims) of average feature-space density.

Raises:

ValueError – If either features or global_sim_num is not set

get_features(standardize=True, file_fmt=None, **kwargs)

Calculates and sets the features for the current formalism and input file. Also gets and sets filenames, global simulation numbers, local simulation numbers, and atom ids for the current formalism and input_file.

Parameters:

  • standardize (bool | None (default: True)) – True if the features need to be standardized using StandardScalar

  • file_fmt (str | None (default: None)) – File format for the structures to be investigated. Only neccessary if the file format does not match the default file format of the current formalism. The files will be converted to support the current set formalism. If formalism is GAP, structures and file_fmt must be in a support pyRANN format. See “load” for supported formats.

  • **kwargs – Any other necessary arguments needed for the current formalism. Currently, the only time this should be used is to provide the element symbol(s) if the formalism uses soap descriptors (i.e. symbol=’Mg’).

Notes

features, filenames, global_sim_num, local_id, and local_sim_num attributes are set after calling this function.

get_umap(**kwargs)

Initializes UMAP for the set features.

Parameters:

**kwargs – Any UMAP arguments. pyRANN defaults use UMAP defaults except for the following: verbose=True, init=”pca”, n_neighbors=54. If kwargs are defined, all undefined UMAP args will default back to UMAP default.

Returns:

An instance of UMAP to visualize the descriptor space

Raises:

ValueError – If the features are not set

Notes

Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction (UMAP) [1]. Please see https://umap-learn.readthedocs.io/en/latest/

References

[1]

McInnes, L, Healy, J, UMAP: Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection for Dimension Reduction, ArXiv e-prints 1802.03426, 2018

plot(mapper, color_data, hover_info, color_type='categorical', rescale_colorbar=False, cmap='Viridis256', subset_points=None, key_str=None)

Saves an interactive bokeh html plot to visualize the feature-space.

Parameters:

  • mapper – UMAP instance

  • color_data (ArrayLike) – The data that determines how each data point in the interactive plot is colored.

  • hover_info (dict) – Dictionary containing the information that will appear when a data point is hovered over in the interactive plot. The key will be the label and the values should be arrays of length natoms.

  • color_type (str | None (default: 'categorical')) – Type of coloring to use. If categorical, each unique item in color_data gets its own color. If linear, viridis colormap will be used to color the data from min(color_data) to max(color_data). Defaults to categorical.

  • rescale_colorbar (bool | None (default: False)) – Boolean to rescale colorbar when changing the visible range on the plot if color_type == ‘linear’. Ignored if color_type == ‘categorical’.

  • cmap (str | None (default: 'Viridis256')) – String representation of desiried 256 color palette from Bokeh.

  • subset_points (ArrayLike | None (default: None)) – 1-D array of booleans of length natoms. True means that the data point for that atom should be plotted.

  • key_str (str | None (default: None)) – The string to name the html file.

Notes

Todo

  • Change method to where rescale_colorbar, cmap are kwargs.

  • Add in kwargs for plot size and background fill color.

reduce(percent=50.0, metric=None, **kwargs)

Reduces the database by ${percent}%. The default metric is to delete simulations in the most dense regions of the fingerprint space.

Parameters:

  • percent (float (default: 50.0)) – The percentage of data to delete.

  • metric (Callable | None (default: None)) – If given, this must be a user defined function that takes in values and deletes a certain amount of data.

  • **kwargs – Any additional arguments that the user defined function needs.

Return type:

ndarray

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of global simulation numbers to keep in the database.

property features: ndarray

Returns the features

Returns:

np.ndarray – Features of shape (natoms, nfeatures)

property formalism: str

Returns the current formalism.

Returns:

str – The current formalism.

property global_sim_num: ndarray

Returns array of the global simulation number each atom belongs to.

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of global simulation numbers each atom belongs to.

property input_file: str

Returns a string representation of the input file.

Returns:

str – A string representation of the input file.

property local_id: ndarray

Returns the local (within-simulation) atom id for each atom.

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of local atom ids.

property local_sim_num: ndarray

Returns 1-D array of local (within-file) simulation numbers for each atom.

Returns:

np.ndarray – 1-D array of local simulation numbers for each atom

calibration

Calibration module. Run a = pyrann.calibration.PairRANN(‘input_file’) then a.setup() to have the c++ code calculate fingerprints for all dump files in input file’s dumpdirectory. The fingerprints can then be accessed by using the following classes and methods.

Class

PairRANN

Class for accessing RANN calibration code.

Simulation

Class for accessing some per-simulation properties.
class PairRANN

Bases: pybind11_object

Class for accessing RANN calibration code. To use methods, you must set a variable equal to calibration.PairRANN(‘input_file’) first. Then run methods on the chosen variable.

Attributes

natoms

Total number of atoms read.

nsims

Total number of simulations read.

sims_per_file

An array of simulations per dump file.

Methods

feature(…)

Array of fingerprint (non-normalized) values for a given simulation (global) for a given atom id (local).

finish(…)

Clean up after running calibration code.

firstneigh(…)

Array of neighbors for a given atom id (local) in a given simulation (global).

force(…)

Array of forces for a given simulation (global) for a given atom id (local).

id(…)

Array of atom id's (local) for a given simulation number (global).

run(…)

Run Calibration code to train a RANN potential.

setup(…)

Set up RANN calibration code.

types(…)

Array of atom types for a given simulation (global).
feature(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN, sim: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex, atom: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.float64]

Array of fingerprint (non-normalized) values for a given simulation (global) for a given atom id (local). PairRANN.feature(sim_num, atom_id).

Parameters:

  • sim (int) – Global simulation number.

  • atom (int) – Local atom id.

Returns:

Array of non-normalized fingerprint values for a given local atom id in a given global simulation number.

Usage:

calibration.PairRANN.feature(global_sim_num, local_atom_id)

finish(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN) → None

Clean up after running calibration code. calibration.PairRANN.finish().

firstneigh(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN, sim: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex, atom: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.int32]

Array of neighbors for a given atom id (local) in a given simulation (global). PairRANN.firstneigh(sim_num, atom_id).

Parameters:

  • sim (int) – Global simulation number.

  • atom (int) – Local atom id.

Returns:

Array of atom id’s for the neighbors for a given local atom id in a given global simulation number.

Usage:

calibration.PairRANN.firstneigh(global_sim_num, local_atom_id)

force(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN, sim: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex, atom: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.float64]

Array of forces for a given simulation (global) for a given atom id (local). PairRANN.force(sim_num, atom_id).

Parameters:

  • sim (int) – Global simulation number.

  • atom (int) – Local atom id.

Returns:

Array of forces for a given local atom id in a given global simulation number.

Usage:

calibration.PairRANN.force(global_sim_num, local_atom_id)

id(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN, sim: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.int32]

Array of atom id’s (local) for a given simulation number (global). PairRANN.id(sim_num).

Parameters:

sim (int) – Global simulation number.

Returns:

Array of local atom id’s in a given global simulation number.

Usage:

calibration.PairRANN.id(global_sim_num)

run(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN) → None

Run Calibration code to train a RANN potential. Ending training early using ctrl+c. The code checks for this command every 10 epochs. calibration.PairRANN.run().

setup(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN) → None

Set up RANN calibration code. Use this to obtain fingerprint values. calibration.PairRANN.setup().

types(self: pyrann.calibration.PairRANN, sim: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.int32]

Array of atom types for a given simulation (global). PairRANN.types(sim_num).

Parameters:

sim (int) – Global simulation number.

Returns:

Array of atom types in a given global simulation number.

Usage:

calibration.PairRANN.types(global_sim_num)

property natoms

Total number of atoms read. calibration.PairRANN.natoms.

property nsims

Total number of simulations read. calibration.PairRANN.nsims.

property sims_per_file

An array of simulations per dump file. Array is ordered in the order the calibration code reads dump files (see calibration code std::out or Simulation class filename property for order). calibration.PairRANN.sims_per_file.

class Simulation

Bases: pybind11_object

Class for accessing some per-simulation properties. Once setup() has been run, this can be used. setup() produces a list of simulation objects

Attributes

energy

Energy of a simulation.

filename

Filename for a simulation number.

inum

Number of atoms.

types

Array of atom types for a simulation.

Methods

feature(…)

Array of fingerprint (non-normalized) values for a given atom.
feature(self: pyrann.calibration.Simulation, pair: LAMMPS_NS::PairRANN, atom: SupportsInt | SupportsIndex) → numpy.typing.NDArray[numpy.float64]

Array of fingerprint (non-normalized) values for a given atom. a[sim_num].feature(atom_id) is an array of fingerprints for atom atom_id (local) in simulation sim_num (global)

Parameters:

atom (int) – Local atom id to retrieve fingerprint values.

Returns:

Array of non-normalized fingerprint values for given local atom id in given glocal simulation number.

Usage:

a = calibration.PairRANN.setup(‘input_file’) -> a[global_sim_num].feature(local_atom_id).

property energy

Energy of a simulation. a[sim_num].energy is the energy of the global simulation number sim_num.

property filename

Filename for a simulation number. a[sim_num].filename is the dump file filename for global simulation number sim_num.

property inum

Number of atoms. a[sim_num].inum is the number of atoms for global simulation number sim_num.

property types

Array of atom types for a simulation. a[sim_num].types is the array of atom types for global simulation number sim_num
